attributes.stem:
A 25-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic seeking pre-travel advice. He is planning a three-month backpacking trip with his girlfriend through South America, including Bolivia and Argentina. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His immunisations are up to date. He has heard about Chagas disease and some of its potential long-term problems. He is concerned about contracting the infection during their travels.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. No abnormalities are detected on physical examination.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most effective strategy to prevent infection with Trypanosoma cruzi?
options: Avoiding consumption of unpasteurised dairy products
attributes.explanation:
Chagas disease is caused by infection with Trypanosoma cruzi. Whilst Chagas disease is endemic to South America, with changing migration patterns, the disease is now being seen in various non-endemic parts of the world, including Australia. Chagas disease is caused by the protozoan parasite Trypanosoma cruzi, which is transmitted primarily by triatomine bugs (also known as 'kissing bugs'). These insects typically live in the cracks and crevices of poorly constructed homes, particularly those with mud walls, thatched roofs, or adobe structures common in rural areas of Latin America. The cycle of spread starts with the Triatomine vector taking a blood meal from an infected individual, often near the lips and other mucous membrane (hence kissing bugs). The parasite then travels through the vector's gut and is excreted in the faeces. When the vector then seeks another blood meal - from an uninfected individual - faeces are excreted at the site where the blood was drawn and the trypanosomes can enter the wound, infecting this person.

Sleeping in well-constructed accommodation is the most effective prevention strategy because triatomine bugs typically inhabit cracks in walls and roofs of substandard housing. These bugs are nocturnal and feed on blood while people sleep. By staying in well-constructed accommodations with plastered walls, solid roofs, and no structural gaps, travelers can significantly reduce their risk of exposure to these vectors. This represents true vector avoidance, which is the cornerstone of Chagas disease prevention.

Using DEET-containing insect repellent provides some protection against triatomine bugs but is not as effective as avoiding exposure to the vector's habitat altogether. While insect repellents are useful supplementary measures, they do not address the primary mode of transmission, which occurs when the bug defaecates near the bite site and parasites enter through the wound when scratched.

Prophylactic antiparasitic medication is not recommended for Chagas disease prevention. Unlike malaria, there is no established chemoprophylaxis for Chagas disease. Medications like benznidazole and nifurtimox are used for treatment after infection, not for prevention.

Avoiding consumption of unpasteurised dairy products is not relevant for Chagas disease prevention. This would be appropriate advice for preventing other infections like brucellosis or listeriosis, but T. cruzi is not transmitted through dairy products.

Boiling all drinking water would not prevent Chagas disease as the parasite is not waterborne. This measure would help prevent enteric infections caused by bacteria, viruses, or parasites transmitted through contaminated water, but not Chagas disease.

Additional preventive measures for travellers to endemic areas include using bed nets (preferably those treated with insecticides), wearing long-sleeved clothing treated with permethrin, and inspecting sleeping quarters for signs of triatomine bugs. It is also worth noting that Chagas disease can be transmitted through blood transfusions, organ transplantation, and congenitally, though these routes are less relevant for most travellers and are rare sources of transmission.
learningPoints: Chagas disease is caused by Trypanosoma cruzi and transmitted by triatomine bugs that typically inhabit cracks in walls and roofs of poorly constructed homes.
Vector avoidance through proper accommodation selection is the most effective prevention strategy for Chagas disease.
Insect repellents containing DEET, permethrin-treated clothing, and bed nets provide additional protection against triatomine bugs.
references: Perez-Molina JA, Molina I. Chagas disease. Lancet. 2018; 391:82- 94. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28673423/
Hochberg NS, Montgomery SP. Chagas disease. Annals of Internal Medicine.  2023; 176:17-32.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10442057/
Bern C. Chagas' disease. The New England Journal of Medicine. 2015; 373:456-466.  Available at: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMra1410150
